reason: Both answers hit the same core points (change the axis, weaponize incumbent's bloat, own a niche/metric, get to buyers before the RFP forms, qualify out of unfavorable bake-offs) and are similarly structured and concise. B is marginally sharper: it gives a more concrete example for weaponizing bloat ("80% of features go unused... paying for it in seat price"), a crisper reframe line ("which problem do we actually have?"), and explicitly names the underserved buyer (developer vs. procurement/platform team) as the target for reframing, which more directly nails the "target buyers for whom the checklist is irrelevant" key point. A is solid and adds a nice attribution to April Dunford, but its buyer-targeting point is more diffuse. Difference is small but B edges ahead on precision and actionability.
